questions.question: In a Linux environment, what is the specific state of a process that is temporarily unable to continue because it is waiting for an external event, such as a disk I/O operation or a network packet?

answerOptions.text: Blocked
answerOptions.rationale: The blocked state occurs when a process is suspended while waiting for an external resource or operation to complete before it can move back to the ready queue.

answerOptions.text: Ready
answerOptions.rationale: In this state, the process is fully prepared to execute and is simply waiting for the CPU scheduler to allocate processing time.

answerOptions.text: Running
answerOptions.rationale: This state indicates that the process is currently being executed by the CPU.

answerOptions.text: Zombie
answerOptions.rationale: A zombie process has completed execution but still has an entry in the process table to allow the parent to read its exit status.

questions.hint: Consider the term used when a process's progress is halted by external dependencies.

questions.question: Which of the following describes a key architectural difference between containers and Virtual Machines (VMs)?

answerOptions.text: Containers share the host operating system kernel, whereas VMs include a full guest operating system.
answerOptions.rationale: Containerization provides OS-level virtualization by sharing the host kernel, making containers more lightweight and faster to start than VMs.

answerOptions.text: VMs share the host kernel to provide better isolation than containers.
answerOptions.rationale: VMs are isolated by a hypervisor and do not share the host kernel; they run their own independent guest kernels.

answerOptions.text: Containers require a hypervisor like VMware or VirtualBox to manage resource abstraction.
answerOptions.rationale: Hypervisors are used for hardware-level virtualization in VMs, while containers use a container engine to interact with the host OS.

answerOptions.text: VMs use Docker engines to package applications and their dependencies together.
answerOptions.rationale: Docker is a containerization platform, not a virtualization technology used to run full guest virtual machines.

questions.hint: Think about which technology is 'lighter' because it reuses the underlying system's core.

questions.question: When following a standard Git workflow, what is the primary purpose of a 'Pull Request' (or Merge Request)?

answerOptions.text: To propose changes and facilitate code review before merging into a target branch.
answerOptions.rationale: Pull requests allow team members to examine code, discuss improvements, and ensure quality before changes are integrated into the main codebase.

answerOptions.text: To download the entire repository from a remote server to a local machine.
answerOptions.rationale: Downloading the repository to a local machine is performed by the 'git clone' command.

answerOptions.text: To save local changes and record them in the project history with a descriptive message.
answerOptions.rationale: Recording local changes is the function of the 'git commit' command.

answerOptions.text: To push local branch updates directly to the production environment without verification.
answerOptions.rationale: Directly pushing to production bypasses the collaborative review and testing intended by the pull request process.

questions.hint: It serves as a collaborative checkpoint before code integration.

questions.question: At which layer of the OSI model does the Internet Protocol (IP) operate, and what is its main function?

answerOptions.text: Network Layer; handles routing and logical addressing.
answerOptions.rationale: The Network Layer (Layer 3) is responsible for determining the best physical path for data and managing IP addresses.

answerOptions.text: Transport Layer; ensures reliable end-to-end data delivery.
answerOptions.rationale: The Transport Layer (Layer 4) manages protocols like TCP and UDP for data flow and error correction.

answerOptions.text: Data Link Layer; manages MAC addresses and physical node-to-node links.
answerOptions.rationale: The Data Link Layer (Layer 2) handles hardware-level addressing and error detection between adjacent nodes.

answerOptions.text: Application Layer; provides the interface for end-user software.
answerOptions.rationale: The Application Layer (Layer 7) handles high-level protocols like HTTP and SMTP that users directly interact with.

questions.hint: Think about the layer responsible for finding the 'route' to a destination.

questions.question: According to the CAP theorem for distributed systems, if an engineer chooses to prioritize Availability and Partition Tolerance ($AP$), what must they sacrifice?

answerOptions.text: Consistency
answerOptions.rationale: The CAP theorem states that a distributed system can only guarantee two out of the three properties; prioritizing A and P means data may not be identical across all nodes immediately.

answerOptions.text: Latency
answerOptions.rationale: While latency is a performance metric, it is not one of the three fundamental trade-offs defined by the CAP theorem.

answerOptions.text: Security
answerOptions.rationale: Security is a system requirement but is independent of the mathematical trade-offs between consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.

answerOptions.text: Throughput
answerOptions.rationale: Throughput refers to the volume of data processed, which is a performance characteristic rather than a CAP theorem pillar.

questions.hint: Recall the three letters that make up the acronym of the theorem.

questions.question: Which Linux command would a DevOps engineer use to change file permissions so that the owner has full access (read, write, execute) while all other users have no access?

answerOptions.text: chmod 700
answerOptions.rationale: The digit 7 represents the sum of read (4), write (2), and execute (1), while 0 removes all permissions for the group and others.

answerOptions.text: chmod 777
answerOptions.rationale: This command grants full read, write, and execute permissions to the owner, group, and the general public.

answerOptions.text: chmod 644
answerOptions.rationale: This setting allows the owner to read and write, while others can only read the file.

answerOptions.text: chmod 755
answerOptions.rationale: This grants full access to the owner but limits the group and others to only reading and executing the file.

questions.hint: Numeric permissions follow the order: Owner, Group, Others.

questions.question: What is the specific role of 'Docker Compose' in a containerized development environment?

answerOptions.text: To define and manage multi-container applications using a single YAML configuration file.
answerOptions.rationale: Docker Compose allows developers to orchestrate the startup and networking of multiple interdependent services on a single host.

answerOptions.text: To provide a secure registry for storing and versioning container images.
answerOptions.rationale: Storing and versioning images is typically handled by services like Docker Hub or Amazon ECR.

answerOptions.text: To automatically scale containers across a cluster of many physical servers.
answerOptions.rationale: Scaling across multiple nodes is a task for orchestration platforms like Kubernetes or Docker Swarm.

answerOptions.text: To compile source code into a binary before it is placed into a Docker image.
answerOptions.rationale: Compiling source code is part of the build process, often defined within a Dockerfile rather than Docker Compose.

questions.hint: It simplifies running a group of containers that need to work together on one machine.

questions.question: In a basic CI/CD pipeline, which stage is primarily focused on compiling code and creating artifacts such as Docker images or binaries?

answerOptions.text: Build
answerOptions.rationale: The build stage is the initial phase where source code is transformed into a deployable format.

answerOptions.text: Test
answerOptions.rationale: The test stage involves running automated scripts to verify that the newly built artifact functions correctly.

answerOptions.text: Deploy
answerOptions.rationale: The deployment stage involves moving the verified artifact into a target environment like staging or production.

answerOptions.text: Monitor
answerOptions.rationale: Monitoring is a post-deployment phase focused on observing the application's health and performance in real-time.

questions.hint: This stage creates the actual 'product' from the raw code.

questions.question: How does 'Space Sharing' resource management differ from 'Time Sharing'?

answerOptions.text: Space sharing involves dedicating a resource to one entity until it is explicitly deleted, whereas time sharing rotates resources sequentially.
answerOptions.rationale: Space sharing is used for static resources like disk storage where space is held by the user, while time sharing alternates CPU usage among multiple tasks.

answerOptions.text: Space sharing refers to multiple users accessing the same CPU cycle simultaneously.
answerOptions.rationale: Simultaneous CPU cycle usage is not physically possible for a single core; this is usually handled by rapid time-sliced rotation (time sharing).

answerOptions.text: Time sharing is exclusively used for cloud storage, while space sharing is for virtual machines.
answerOptions.rationale: Storage management typically follows space sharing principles regardless of whether it is cloud-based or local.

answerOptions.text: Space sharing is a manual process, while time sharing is always automated by the OS kernel.
answerOptions.rationale: Both resource management strategies are automated functions of the operating system's kernel and scheduler.

questions.hint: Think about the difference between owning a plot of land versus sharing a public transit seat.

questions.question: In a 3-Tier Architecture, which layer is responsible for processing business rules, making logical decisions, and performing calculations?

answerOptions.text: Application Layer (Logic Tier)
answerOptions.rationale: The middle tier serves as the 'brain' of the application, processing requests from the presentation layer and interacting with the data tier.

answerOptions.text: Presentation Layer
answerOptions.rationale: The presentation layer is the top tier, primarily focused on the user interface and how information is displayed to the user.

answerOptions.text: Data Storage Layer
answerOptions.rationale: The data tier is responsible for the persistent storage and retrieval of information, typically using a database.

answerOptions.text: Physical Layer
answerOptions.rationale: The physical layer is a networking concept (Layer 1) and is not part of the standard application architecture tiers.

questions.hint: It is the tier that sits between the user interface and the database.

questions.question: What metacharacter is used in Regular Expressions (Regex) as a shorthand to match any single numerical digit from $0$ to $9$?

answerOptions.rationale: The shorthand sequence \\d represents the set of all digits, effectively functioning as [0-9].
This metacharacter matches 'word' characters, which include alphanumeric characters and underscores.
This metacharacter is used to identify whitespace characters such as spaces, tabs, and newlines.
This is a boundary metacharacter used to match the position between a word character and a non-word character.

questions.hint: The letter used in this metacharacter is the first letter of the word 'digit'.

questions.question: Why is Network Address Translation (NAT) a critical component for most modern home and office routers?

answerOptions.text: It allows multiple devices with private IP addresses to share a single public IP address to access the internet.
answerOptions.rationale: NAT translates internal private addresses to a public one, conserving the limited pool of available IPv4 addresses.

answerOptions.text: It provides end-to-end encryption for all data packets traveling across the local area network.
answerOptions.rationale: Encryption is typically handled by protocols like TLS or IPsec, while NAT is purely an address mapping process.

answerOptions.text: It replaces the need for a DNS server by automatically resolving domain names to MAC addresses.
answerOptions.rationale: Domain name resolution is handled by DNS, and IP-to-MAC resolution is handled by ARP; NAT does not perform these roles.

answerOptions.text: It increases the physical speed of the internet connection by compressing incoming data packets.
answerOptions.rationale: NAT does not compress data; its primary function is modifying the IP header information for routing purposes.

questions.hint: Think about how many devices in your house use the internet despite having only one connection from your ISP.
